subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Growth Modeling: Bacterial Population, Competing Models & Semi-Log Analysis
stimulus:
A biologist is studying a colony of bacteria growing in a nutrient-rich solution. The biologist measures the population at selected times and records the following data:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Population $P$ (thousands) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 3.1 |
| 2 | 7.4 |
| 5 | 28.6 |
| 8 | 110.3 |
| 11 | 421.7 |

The biologist considers two competing models for the population: an exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$ and a power model $W(t) = c \cdot t^d$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$ to write two equations that can be used to find the values of constants $a$ and $b$ in the exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$.

(ii) Find the values of $a$ and $b$ as decimal approximations. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Two correct equations — $a \cdot b^0 = 3.1$, which gives $a = 3.1$; and $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7$; equivalently $b^{11} = \frac{421.7}{3.1} = 136.03\ldots$

1 pt: Correct values $a = 3.1$ and $b = 136.03^{1/11} \approx 1.563$ (from $b = \left(\frac{421.7}{3.1}\right)^{1/11} = e^{\ln(136.03)/11} \approx e^{0.4466} \approx 1.563$; accept values in $[1.558, 1.568]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step
parts.scoring_notes: The first equation gives $a = 3.1$ directly since $b^0 = 1$. For $b$: $\ln b = \frac{\ln(421.7/3.1)}{11} = \frac{\ln(136.03)}{11} \approx \frac{4.913}{11} \approx 0.4466$, so $b \approx e^{0.4466} \approx 1.563$. Accept values in $[1.558, 1.568]$. Note: a student using a different pair of data points will get a slightly different $b$; accept any two-point derivation with correct algebra and a value reasonably close to 1.563.

parts.prompt:
(i) To determine whether the exponential model $E$ is more appropriate than a linear model for this data, a biologist creates a semi-log plot by graphing $\ln(P)$ versus $t$. Using the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$, calculate the slope of the line on the semi-log plot.

(ii) Explain what it means for the semi-log plot to appear approximately linear. If instead the data appeared curved (concave up) on the semi-log plot, what would that indicate about the growth rate of the bacterial population?
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct slope — $\frac{\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1)}{11 - 0} = \frac{6.043 - 1.131}{11} = \frac{4.912}{11} \approx 0.4465$ (accept values in $[0.444, 0.449]$); supporting work must show a quotient using $\ln$ of the population values

1 pt: Two-part explanation — (a) linear appearance on the semi-log plot confirms the exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$ is appropriate, because taking $\ln$ of both sides gives $\ln(E) = \ln(a) + t \ln(b)$, a linear function of $t$; AND (b) a concave-up curve on the semi-log plot would indicate the growth rate is accelerating faster than any simple exponential model — the population is growing super-exponentially (e.g., the per-capita growth rate itself is increasing over time)
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\ln(421.7) \approx 6.043$ and $\ln(3.1) \approx 1.131$, giving slope $\approx 4.912/11 \approx 0.4465$. Note this equals $\ln(b)$ from part (a) (consistent). For part (ii), the first component (linearity confirms exponential) requires connecting $\ln(ab^t) = \ln a + t\ln b$ to the linearity of the semi-log plot. The second component (concave up implies super-exponential) is a higher-order reasoning question — accept any description that correctly identifies accelerating growth beyond exponential, such as 'the model underestimates growth' or 'the doubling time is decreasing.'

parts.prompt:
(i) The biologist's colleague proposes that the population exceeds 500 thousand bacteria sometime between $t = 11$ and $t = 12$ hours. Using the exponential model $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a), write and solve an inequality to find the exact time $t$ at which the population first exceeds 500 thousand. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.

(ii) The colleague also suggests a linear model $L(t) = mt + 3.1$ fitted through $t = 0$ and $t = 11$. Find the value of $m$ as a decimal approximation. Then explain, using the structure of the data table, why the linear model is less appropriate than the exponential model for this data set.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct inequality and solution — $3.1 \cdot b^t > 500 \Rightarrow b^t > \frac{500}{3.1} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(500/3.1)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{\ln(161.29)}{\ln(1.563)} \approx \frac{5.084}{0.4466} \approx 11.38$ hours (accept values in $[11.3, 11.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup and logarithm step

1 pt: Correct slope $m = \frac{421.7 - 3.1}{11 - 0} = \frac{418.6}{11} \approx 38.05$ (accept values in $[38.0, 38.1]$) AND a valid explanation — the ratios of successive population values in the data table are approximately constant (e.g., $7.4/3.1 \approx 2.39$, $28.6/7.4 \approx 3.86$, $110.3/28.6 \approx 3.86$, $421.7/110.3 \approx 3.82$), which is the signature of exponential (not linear) growth; or equivalently, the differences are not constant (differences are $4.3$, $21.2$, $81.7$, $311.4$), ruling out linear
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the exact threshold time depends on the value of $b$ from part (a). Using $b \approx 1.563$: $\ln(500/3.1) / \ln(1.563) = \ln(161.29)/0.4466 \approx 5.084/0.4466 \approx 11.38$ hours. Award the point for correct inequality setup and correct application of logarithms even if $b$ differs slightly from the expected value due to rounding. For part (ii), the explanation must explicitly reference either: (a) the approximately constant ratios between consecutive data values (characteristic of exponential growth), OR (b) the rapidly increasing differences (ruling out linear). A response that only states 'exponential fits better' without reference to the data structure does not earn the point.
